All of the interactive setup for our levels happens in  
what we call the Level Editor. In this section we will  
cover the following topics:  
TFB Tool  
Handbook  
Opening the level editor  
Exploring the tool UI  
Creating a simple level with mesh, a player, and  
camera control  
Running the level  
Live updating

available for inspection at C:\tfb\content\levels  
\test\Tutorial\Chapter_02\Tutorial_Ch2.lvl

Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
The TFB Level Editor  
Launching the Tool  
Locate the Level Editor executable at C:\tfb\bin\tfbtool.exe (you probably will want  
to create a shortcut to this guy - you'll be launching it a lot!) and run it.  
The tool will launch and prompt you to open a level. We're going to be creating a new  
level, so just hit Cancel.  
You may be prompted to update - that is the tool's built-in source control, which will be  
discussed later - but just hit Don't Update for now.  
The tool will then load up your new, empty level.  
NOTE: The reason the tool needs to load stuﬀ for an empty level is because the  
game always builds the global level which contains data we want to use across  
the whole game. We'll talk about that more, later.  
Interface Explanation  
Once the load is complete, you should be presented with a screen that looks roughly  
like this and contains four main areas (photo-chopping applied on my end to make  
things ﬁt in a small apce)  
2 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
The various panels (except for area 2) may be torn oﬀ or rearranged however you like.  
Area 1 is the Message Panel, which contains the message/build information area  
as well as the script searching tools.  
Area 2 is the Level View, where you can see the 3D world view/placement  
/navigation section.  
Area 3 is the Level Panel, where you deﬁne the level mesh and includes as well  
as create all non-actor placements/objects like sounds, particles, etc.  
Area 4 is the Actor Panel, where you may create actors (an object that has a  
presence in the 3d world) and assign scripts and/or models to them.  
We will delve into what the purpose of all these panels are in due time, but for now,  
let's start by assigning some level mesh.  
Assigning Mesh  
For the purposes of this guide, we will use some very ugly mesh I stole from another  
designer a while ago - the guide will not cover creation of mesh in Maya, but feel free  
to use your own mesh, if you wish.  
On the Files sub-tab of the Level Settings tab, click the big area that says NONE to  
bring up a ﬁle dialog to select some mesh.  
3 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Pick the dang_test.ma ﬁle in C:\tfb\content\levels\test\Tutorial\dang_test.ma  
The mesh will load and export, and, voila, you have level mesh.  
Navigation  
Now that we have some level mesh, we can start using the 3D view of the level editor  
to navigate around. Navigation is done in a Maya-like fashion as follows:  
Left click: Select an object  
Double middle click: Set focal point  
Mousewheel in/out: Zoom in/out based upon focal point  
Alt+Left click: Rotate view around focal point  
Alt+Middle click: Pan view  
When you load up dang_test.ma, you will likely be looking at (0,0,0) at a low angle.  
Use the controls above to zoom out and rotate until you have more of a top-down view.  
NOTE: All those objects you see in the below image (and in your tool view) are  
from the included global level - they won't show up when you run, don't worry!  
4 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Getting Started  
Now that we have some mesh in, let's actually create and place something in the world.  
Creating an Actor  
Double middle-click somewhere to set the focal point (you'll see the little 3-pronged  
axis move to indicate this), and then press New on the Actor Panel to create a new  
actor there.  
An actor named New Actor will be created, but you can rename him. Let's call him  
Player Actor. A red and white cone, which is how we represent actors without  
models, will appear in the 3D view representing the position of the actor in world  
space.  
The arrowed axis handles are used for manipulating things in the world view. Try  
moving the actor around the level with the handles:  
The blue and red arrows move objects on Z and X coordinates.  
The green arrow moves objects up and down on the Y coordinate.  
The purple ball moves an object along the ground - it will retain its height from  
the ground and track the collision mesh.  
Assigning a Model  
A player without a model is hardly exciting, so let's assign a model to the actor. In the  
Actor Panel, make sure your Player Actor is still highlighted and press the big NONE  
button next to Model to open a ﬁle selection dialog for model assignment.  
5 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Let's pick the AirDragon.mdl model which is in C;\tfb\content\models\characters\  
You may be asked if you want to use the model's collision/movement properties. Say  
No.  
NOTE: Later, we'll explore more about mdl ﬁles, the TFB Tool's package for 3d  
objects.  
You will now have a shiny Air Dragon in the 3d view.  
Also note that this actor now has a Character Movement tab on the Actor Panel.  
Only actors with models are allowed to run physics.  
By default, new actors with models have gravity - we want that! Since our Player Actor  
has gravity, it will need a collision volume so it does not fall through the ground. Go  
into the Collision tab and do the following:  
Change the shape to a sphere.  
Set the width to something reasonably larger, say 6 feet.  
You can preview the collision volume by checking the Show box.  
It should look something like this when you're done:

Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Saving our New Level  
Since we've made a bit of progress, it would serve us well to actually save the level in case  
of a crash, because it's necessary when we ﬁnally build the level (the system needs to know  
what to call the build), and just because saving is cool and awesome, just like you.  
Select Save from the File menu.  
Navigate to C:\tfb\content\levels\test\Tutorial\ and save it as Tutorial.lvl  
Preparing to Run  
We're almost up and running with something playable! We just need to tell the game that  
this model is a player and create some script to manage the camera.  
7 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Making a Player  
The way we tell the game that an actor should be a player is done, as you will see with  
almost all game logic, in script! Being a player means the following things:  
The actor can activate and deactivate things with activation ranges - more on that  
later.  
The actor will move in response to the controller's joystick.  
The actor will automatically jump in response to the controller's primary button press,  
if it has a Jump Init Speed.  
Now, let's assign a script to the Player Actor and tell it to be a player!  
Assigning a Script  
Select the Player Actor and click the New button next to the Script line in the  
Basics tab of the Actor Panel  
A ﬁle dialog will appear asking you where to save the new script. Make a subdirectory  
in the Tutorial folder by clicking the new directory button.  
Name the new directory scripts.  
Enter the directory and name the new script Player  
8 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
At this point, the actor panel will change to represent the assigned script (the box will  
no longer say NONE) and a new script window will pop up.  
NOTE: In this step you made a new scripts directory. Though the tool does not  
enforce any directory structure, it's a good practice to place scripts for your level  
in <LevelFolder>/scripts just for organization and because the tool will  
automatically try to navigate to <LevelMesh.ma>/scripts when you create or  
look for subsequent scripts.  
Setting the Player Controller  
We'll get into the in-depth details of scripting later, but for now, let's just write one simple  
line to make the actor behave as a player. Your new script window, at this point, should look  
something like this:  
Expand the STARTUP section by clicking the little plus sign.  
Open the new OpCode menu by right clicking on the area within that says ﬂow  
continue and select the set *reference command.  
9 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Adjust the lefthand parameter (denoted by the dark gray box surrounding the words)  
by left clicking on it and select my > *controller.  
Adjust the righthand parameter to (CONTROLLERS) > *controller 1.  
Your completed script should look like this.  
Now save the script by going to File > Save or hitting Ctrl+S, and close the script.

Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Creating a Camera  
Though we could feasibly run the game right now, we need a bit of logic to tell the game  
camera, also called the viewport, what to look at. This section will get into some scripting  
techniques which are a little more advanced than I'd like to discuss at the present, but the  
camera is a necessary evil for our little game. I will utilize a script I've already written just  
get you up and running.  
Create a new actor and name it Camera Actor.  
Assign a script to the actor by clicking the NONE area and picking the script at C:\tfb  
\content\levels\test\Tutorial\Templates\scripts\camera.ai  
The Camera Script  
Without walking you through the steps to get this script going, I'd like to walk you through  
each of the 4 lines in plain English.  
Select the Camera Actor and click the Edit button to bring up the actor's script.  
Camera Explained  
The script will look something like this.  
Each line is doing the following, respectively:  
11 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
1. Place the viewport (a.k.a. the camera) at myself, meaning the actor running this  
script - the Camera Actor.  
2. Displace the viewport a ﬁxed distance in space, at a given heading (horizontal angle)  
and pitch (vertical angle). Now the camera is up in space some distance from the  
Camera Actor.  
3. Point the viewport's facing to be the Camera Actor's heading from it. Now the  
viewport will face the Camera Actor.  
4. Point the viewport's tilt to be the Camera Actor's pitch from it. Now the viewport will  
tilt down to face the Camera Actor in the vertical sense.  
Saving a Copy of the Camera Script  
Down the road, I'd like you to manipulate this script. Let's save oﬀ a copy of it so you can  
play with it.  
Select File > Save As in the script window.  
Save the script as Camera.ai in C:\tfb\content\levels\test\Tutorial\scripts\ (note that  
the ﬁle dialog automatically popped you into the scripts directory you made, as  
mentioned above during the creation of the directory).  
Close down the script window and brace for exciting impact.  
Running The Game  
In the Target menu, select the appropriate console (I'll be using Wii) for your setup.  
12 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
PRESS F5!!! - that's it! This is a shortcut for Build and Baunch, which also lives  
under the Target menu in the tool.  
At this point, the tool will automatically save the level (so saving is never a required step  
when building and launching), then build and prepare the level to be sent to the target  
system. When the build is done, it will launch the level on the target. When that's all done,  
your game should be up and running - you should see a camera angle pointing at our air  
dragon, and moving the stick on the 1st controller should move him around.  
Live Updating  
After building and launching, you should have a live connection to the target and will be  
able to live update placements and scripts, one of the coolest things about our system.  
Try selecting the Camera Actor and dragging it around - you'll notice that the camera  
moves.  
Bring up the Camera Actor's script for editing by selecting it in the Actor Panel and  
clicking the Edit button.  
Try adjusting the length value in the 2nd line (the displace). Hit Ctrl+S (or select  
File > Save from the script editing window) and you'll notice the camera's distance  
changes.  
13 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
Making the Camera Follow the Player  
You may notice that the camera is completely static while your player moves around. We  
can ﬁx that! Let's add a new line to our camera script to make it follow the player around.  
Right click on the topmost line (set value default's distance from myself) to bring  
up the OpCode list and select set value to add a new set value line.  
Left click the lefthand parameter to change it to my > distance.  
Pull down on the 2nd parameter (the part after the from) and change it to (ACTORS)  
> Player Actor > <self>.  
14 of 15  
01/11/2016 03:24 PM  
Ch. 2: Creating a Level - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._2:_Creating_a_Level  
The ﬁnal line should read as follows. Save with Ctrl+S and behold.  
We have changed the camera actor to place itself at the Player Actor's position every  
frame, so all the subsequent camera commands will be centered around the player, since  
the camera actor's position is now being based upon the player's.  
Continue onwards to Chapter 3: Basic Scripting.
